definition: a training approach in which the algorithm chooses some of the data it learns from. active learning is valuable when labeled examples are scarce or expensive. an active learning algorithm selectively seeks the examples it needs for learning.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: software that can reason about multimodal user inputs in order to plan and execute actions on behalf of the user. in reinforcement learning, an agent is the entity that uses a policy to maximize expected return when transitioning between states of the environment.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: AI systems composed of agents that can behave and interact autonomously in order to achieve their objectives.
source: UK Government, 'AI Insights: Agentic AI' (2025)

definition: structured, adversarial testing of models and systems to uncover unsafe behaviors and policy violations, often advocated alongside safe-harbor evaluation norms.
source: ACM DL—Position: a safe harbor for AI evaluation and red teaming (2024)

definition: a sequence of rules that a computer follows to complete a task — it takes an input, for example, from a data set, then performs a test or calculation on it and generates an output. algorithms can be used in this way to spot patterns in data and make predictions.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: decision-making errors or unfair outcomes that can arise from problems with an algorithm’s processing of data, or flaws and biases in the underlying data itself. a bias can result in an algorithm inadvertently privileging or disadvantaging one group of users over another group.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: research aimed at ensuring advanced ai systems pursue goals that align with human values and safety constraints, including avoiding harmful or biased outputs.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a computer system capable of generating new scientific knowledge and performing any task that humans can. it would enable the creation of a superintelligent computer that learns and develops autonomously and can operate across domains. sometimes referred to in popular writing as “god-like ai.”
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: the science of enabling machines to perform tasks that would previously have required human brainpower — for example, reasoning, decision-making, learning from mistakes, predicting outcomes, and problem solving.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a pre-trained model that can serve as the starting point for fine-tuning to address specific tasks or applications.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a model architecture for text representation. a trained bert model can act as part of a larger model for text classification or other ml tasks. bert relies on self-attention, uses the encoder part of the transformer, is bidirectional, and uses masking for unsupervised training.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: stereotyping, prejudice or favoritism towards some things, people, or groups over others. these biases can affect data collection, system design, and user interaction. another meaning is systematic error introduced by sampling or reporting procedures.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: very large data sets that may be analysed computationally to reveal patterns, trends, and associations.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a model whose reasoning is difficult or impossible for humans to understand. humans can observe input and output but not the internal process. most deep models and large language models are considered black boxes.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: the contents of a back-and-forth dialogue with an ml system, typically a large language model. previous interactions form the context for subsequent exchanges.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a software application that can respond to text questions and mimic human conversation by analysing text and predicting appropriate responses.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a natural language processing chatbot driven by ai technology developed by openai. chatgpt is based on a language model that the developer fine-tunes with help from user feedback.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: the computational power required for ai systems to perform tasks, such as processing data, training machine learning models, and making predictions — often measured in floating-point operations per second (flops).
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a field of research that uses computers to obtain useful information from digital images or videos. applications include object recognition, medical imaging, and navigation.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: an embedding that captures syntax, semantics, and context.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: the set of strategies and system design practices for curating, maintaining, and budgeting what information an LLM sees at inference time (beyond just the prompt), including retrieved knowledge, tool definitions, memory, and conversation state to improve reliability and performance.
source: Anthropic—Effective context engineering for AI agents (2025)

definition: an explanation that shows minimal changes to an input that would alter a model’s decision, supporting transparency and user recourse.
source: ACM DL—Counterfactual explanations and algorithmic recourses for machine learning (2025); graph counterfactual explanations survey (2023)

definition: a deep-learning model developed by openai that can generate digital images from text-based natural language descriptions (prompts).
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: obtaining an understanding of data by considering samples, measurement, and visualization. data analysis is useful when a dataset is first received and also in understanding experiments and debugging problems.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: research involving the processing of large amounts of data in order to identify patterns, spot trends and outliers, and provide insights into real-world problems.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a subset of machine learning that can be used to solve complex problems. dl models run on neural networks and can ingest unstructured data such as text, audio, and images.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a neural network containing more than one hidden layer. also called a deep neural network.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: synthetic audio, video or imagery that can convincingly represent a real person, or create a realistic impression of a person who has never existed.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a formal privacy framework that bounds what can be learned about any individual in a dataset by injecting calibrated randomness into computations.
source: ACM DL—Information-theoretic approaches to differential privacy (2023); a survey on differential privacy for unstructured data (2022)

definition: generative models that learn to iteratively denoise samples from noise, enabling high-fidelity synthesis for images, video, and other modalities.
source: ACM DL—Diffusion models: a comprehensive survey (ACM Computing Surveys, 2023)

definition: an open-source document conversion and processing toolkit that parses PDFs (and other formats) into structured representations that can be exported to formats like markdown or json, supporting downstream gen-ai use cases such as dataset creation and retrieval-augmented generation.
source: IBM Research—Docling (2024); Docling GitHub project

definition: an alignment method that directly optimizes a policy using paired human preferences without an explicit reward model.
source: ACM DL—Token-level DPO (2024); β-DPO (2025)

definition: methods and system properties that provide understandable explanations of an AI system’s outputs and behavior so that people can interpret results, appropriately trust them, and manage the system in context.
source: NIST IR 8312—Four Principles of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (2021); OECD AI Principles—Transparency and explainability

definition: a distributed training paradigm where clients collaboratively learn a shared model without centralizing their raw data, often with personalization and privacy mechanisms.
source: ACM DL—Federated learning survey: a multi-level taxonomy of personalization, optimization and robustness (2024)

definition: the unit of measurement used to calculate computational performance (especially for scientific computing and model training/inference).
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a very large pre-trained model trained on a diverse dataset. a foundation model responds well to a wide range of requests and can serve as a base model for customization.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a field in which models create complex, coherent, and original content such as text, images, audio, or video. generative ai is usually associated with large, multimodal models.
source: Google machine learning glossary; The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a machine learning technique that can generate data that is difficult to distinguish from the data it is trained on. a gan is made up of two competing elements: a generator and a discriminator.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a model that creates new examples from a training dataset or determines the probability that a new example comes from the same distribution as the training set.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a family of transformer-based large language models developed by openai since 2018.
source: Google machine learning glossary; The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: the production of plausible but factually incorrect output by a generative ai model.
source: Google machine learning glossary; The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a simple and quickly implemented solution to a problem. heuristics can provide acceptable accuracy but are usually less powerful than deep learning methods.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a type of bias that already exists in the world and appears in datasets. historical bias reflects stereotypes, inequalities, and prejudices in training data.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a system design and operational practice where humans monitor, review, validate, or intervene in an AI system at key stages of development and/or use (sometimes also written as HTL).
source: NIST TN 2287 (2024); OECD—Advancing accountability in AI (2023)

definition: automatic associations or assumptions based on mind models and memories. implicit bias can affect data collection, classification, and system design.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a capability of large language models to adapt to a new task from examples included directly in the prompt at inference time—without updating model weights.
source: ACM DL—The learnability of in-context learning (2023–2024)

definition: the process of making predictions by applying a trained model to new examples. in large language models, inference is generating a response to an input prompt.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: supervised fine-tuning on instruction–response pairs to make models follow natural-language directives more faithfully.
source: ACM DL—A comparative analysis of instruction fine-tuning large language models (2025)

definition: a language model with a very high number of parameters, usually transformer-based, that can recognize, summarize, translate, predict, and generate text.
source: Google machine learning glossary; The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that injects low-rank trainable matrices into existing weights to adapt large models with few additional parameters.
source: ACM DL—LoRA+: efficient low-rank adaptation of large models (2024)

definition: a program or system that trains a model from input data so the model can make predictions from new data; also refers to the field of study concerned with these systems.
source: Google machine learning glossary; The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a sparse architecture that routes tokens to specialized ‘expert’ sub-networks, enabling efficient scaling of large models.
source: ACM DL—Toward efficient inference for mixture of experts (2025); resource-efficient algorithms and systems of foundation models (2024)

definition: a computer system involving multiple, interacting software programs known as agents.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a field that uses computer algorithms to analyse or synthesise human speech and text. modern nlp largely relies on machine learning.
source: Google machine learning glossary; The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a subset of nlp that determines the intentions behind something said or typed. nlu can consider complex aspects like context, sarcasm, and sentiment.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a model containing at least one hidden layer. deep neural networks contain more than one hidden layer. neural networks can model complex nonlinear relationships between features and labels.
source: Google machine learning glossary; The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: software and data that are free to edit and share, to help researchers collaborate, check and replicate findings, and share new developments with the wider community.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a loosely-defined term referring to a pre-trained model that has gone through post-processing such as distillation, fine-tuning, or instruction tuning.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a trained model, now typically referring to large language models or other generative ai systems that can be fine-tuned for specific tasks.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: the initial training of a model on a large dataset. pre-training produces a general model that can be refined through techniques such as distillation or fine-tuning.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: the practice of designing, iterating, and evaluating prompts to steer model behavior and improve reliability.
source: ACM DL—From prompt engineering to prompt science with humans in the loop (2025)

definition: a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that learns small, task-specific prompt vectors or templates while keeping the base model largely frozen.
source: ACM DL—Pre-train, prompt, and predict: a systematic survey of prompting methods (ACM Computing Surveys, 2023)

definition: a technique for improving the quality of large language model output by grounding it with knowledge retrieved after training. rag can increase factual accuracy and support attribution.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: an alignment and fine-tuning approach where human feedback (e.g., rankings or preferences over model outputs) is used to train a reward signal, and the model is then optimized with reinforcement learning to better match human preferences.
source: OpenAI—Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (Ouyang et al., 2022); NeurIPS—Deep reinforcement learning from human preferences (Christiano et al., 2017)

definition: errors in conclusions caused by a selection process that generates systematic differences between samples observed and not observed in data. forms include coverage bias, sampling bias, and non-response bias.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a family of techniques for converting an unsupervised ml problem into a supervised one by creating surrogate labels from unlabeled data.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a variant of self-supervised learning useful when there are far more unlabeled than labeled examples.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: training a model on data where some examples are labeled and others are not.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a game-theoretic approach for attributing a model’s prediction to input features via shapley values, widely used for post-hoc explanation.
source: ACM DL—Explainability for large language models: a survey (2024); WebSHAP (2023)

definition: a currently hypothetical point in time when artificial general intelligence surpasses human intelligence, leading to an acceleration in technological progress and the potential automation of all knowledge-based work.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: an ai system that is self-aware and possesses a higher level of intelligence than humans.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a form of machine learning that uses labelled data to train an algorithm to classify data or predict outcomes accurately.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: techniques that embed detectable statistical signals in generated text to support provenance, detection, or attribution.
source: ACM DL—A survey of text watermarking in the era of large language models (2024)

definition: the atomic unit that a language model trains on and predicts. tokens can be words, characters, or subwords.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a system or algorithm that translates input data into tokens.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: methods that enable models to select and invoke external tools or apis to retrieve information or perform actions, improving reliability and capabilities.
source: ACM DL—A survey on evaluation of large language models (2024)—tool-use coverage; tool learning with foundation models (2024–2025)

definition: a neural network architecture that relies on self-attention to transform sequences of embeddings. transformers may include encoders, decoders, or both, and are the basis of most large language models.
source: Google machine learning glossary

definition: a test of a machine’s ability to demonstrate humanlike intelligence, framed as an evaluator trying to distinguish written responses from a human versus a machine.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: a form of machine learning in which algorithms analyse and cluster unlabeled data sets by looking for hidden patterns in the data — without the need for human intervention to train or correct them.
source: The Alan Turing institute data science and AI glossary

definition: the initial evaluation of a model's quality against a validation set. validation helps guard against overfitting and acts as a first round of testing before evaluation on a test set.
source: Google machine learning glossary
